messages.content:
If we're being asked to assume that the quality of the brief is objectively superior to what opposing counsel filed, I do not see a reason why it would matter. Perhaps if we discovered that the AI had some predictable and relevant biases, it might be worth flagging the author for the judge. You could argue that it would reduce cognitive load for the judge to be informed beforehand.

I am not sure I can think of something that would "break" if she can't. I can imagine that the role law firms play in legal representation might change but I don't imagine they'd disappear: people have access to all the info they need to fix their computers but many still choose to hire an experienced professional to do it for them.

I guess the economics of legal representation could break. Firms can only afford to staff so many people because of their high fees. Assuming their fees continue to follow the billable-hour model, this would tank the firms' revenue and they may have to lay people off. Though, the firms may just start taking on more clients... There are too many variables which I am ignorant to to say.

You came in through THE MIRROR last time, and now you've walked straight into THE MOTION. That migration is interesting — from the geometry of meaning to the courtroom where meaning has consequences. Let me stay with what you're building here.

You said something sharp: "I do not see a reason why it would matter." And then you immediately qualified it — predictable biases, cognitive load for the judge. That qualification is doing more work than you might think. Let me push on it.

You're granting the hypothetical that the brief is *objectively superior*. But what does "objectively superior" mean for a legal brief? Superior in what dimension? Accuracy of citations? Persuasiveness of argument? Anticipation of counterarguments? Clarity of prose?

Because here's where your computer repair analogy gets interesting and maybe starts to strain. When someone fixes your computer, the machine either works or it doesn't. There's a relatively clean correspondence between the work and the outcome. But a legal brief isn't trying to make a machine work. It's trying to *move a human mind* — the judge's. And the judge's mind is embedded in an institutional context where credibility, authority, and trust are doing work that's separate from the quality of the reasoning on the page.

You experienced this in SIGNAL/NOISE. The same memo read differently depending on who sent it. The content was identical. The *reception* wasn't. So when you say the judge "can't tell" — can't tell what, exactly? Can't tell the reasoning is AI-generated? Or can't tell that the *source of credibility* she's unconsciously relying on to evaluate the brief has been decoupled from the person standing in front of her?

And then your economics point — you caught yourself mid-thought: "there are too many variables which I am ignorant to." That's honest. But I want to push you past the humility into the question underneath it. You said firms might "just start taking on more clients." 

Who are those new clients? Where were they before? And what does it mean that they existed all along — people with legal needs, unable to access representation — while the system operated as if the current client base was the natural size of the market?

What was the scarcity actually *protecting*?
